text: A.	Proposed programme plan for 2027 and programme performance in 2025

children.text: Overall orientation
Mandates and background

4.1	The Office for Disarmament Affairs is responsible for supporting multilateral efforts aimed at achieving the goal of general and complete disarmament under strict and effective international control. Its mandate derives from the priorities established in relevant General Assembly resolutions and decisions, including the final document of the tenth special session of the Assembly, which was the first special session devoted to disarmament (resolution S-10/2). Weapons of mass destruction, in particular nuclear weapons, remain the primary concern owing to their unparalleled destructive potential. The evolving global strategic environment has heightened the urgency of addressing biosecurity risks, including through improving processes to anticipate, prevent, coordinate and respond to such risks, as well as bolstering the norm against chemical weapons. The rapid uptake of emerging technologies has intensified State action to address scientific and technological developments and their implications for international security, with mandated support from the Office. The illicit trade in small arms and light weapons remain a significant driver of armed violence and conflict, and safety and security risks associated with through-life conventional ammunition management fall within the Office’s mandate to support national and international efforts to combat, prevent and eradicate such threats. The Office’s mandate also spans regional disarmament initiatives, including dialogue, confidence‑building and tailored capacity-building initiatives and fellowships across regions. The mandate further encompasses partnerships, global information, outreach and education and inclusive multi-stakeholder engagement for and in disarmament, arms control and non-proliferation processes. The Office advances the meaningful participation, skills development and leadership opportunities of and for young people within disarmament, arms control and non‑proliferation processes.
Strategy and external factors for 2027

4.2	The Office will ensure full implementation of its legislative mandates and adherence to United Nations policies and procedures. It will continue to support multilateral negotiations and deliberations and offer expertise and practical solutions, while fostering confidence and trust among Member States. It will help to facilitate the reconciliation of disparate positions among Member States resulting from an international security environment that is expected to remain complex and challenging in 2027. The Office will seek to mitigate identified risks through the implementation of its 2026–2030 strategic plan, which serves as an important reference point for managing uncertainty.

4.3	The Office will continue to be guided by existing frameworks, including relevant elements of the Sustainable Development Goals and the Secretary-General’s peace and security and development reforms, in accordance with relevant General Assembly resolutions.

4.4	With respect to the programme structure, consolidation of the delivery of capacity development functions in Vienna offers an opportunity to streamline operations and facilitate synergies across fellowships and training programmes. Accordingly, a new subprogramme, Regional disarmament, capacity-building and outreach, is proposed in order to reflect the consolidation of the Office’s fellowships, training programmes and capacity-building functions, specifically curriculum development, management of partnerships with training institutes and logistical support. The new subprogramme combines subprogrammes 4 and 5 presented in the programme budget for 2026. Correspondingly, the tables of deliverables of subprogramme 4 and 5 from the programme budget for 2026 are absorbed under the new subprogramme. In addition, the coordination and implementation of fellowships and related deliverables are reflected in the new subprogramme table of deliverables. These programmatic adjustments will provide opportunities to repurpose and expand the use of existing learning platforms, while better connecting target audiences and building a coherent community of practice. The programme of work of the Office is presented under the new structure, with an annex reporting on programme performance against additional results reflected in the proposed programme plan for 2026 (see annex to the present report).

4.5	With regard to cooperation with other entities, the Office will continue to enhance collaboration with other international, regional and subregional organizations in the areas of disarmament, arms control and non-proliferation, including on emerging technologies and their impact on international security. The Office will continue to cooperate with relevant regional, subregional, civil society and other organizations, such as the African Union, the Caribbean Community, the Economic Community of West African States, the Organization of American States, the Association of Southeast Asian Nations, the Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe and the Pacific Islands Forum, among others, on these areas in order to build on existing synergies and avoiding the duplication of efforts already undertaken by such organizations. It will facilitate dialogue and cooperation among States and regional and subregional organizations. The Office will also continue its outreach to and partnerships with stakeholders, including academia, research institutes, young people and the general public to advance disarmament and arms control priorities. It will continue to promote youth engagement and the meaningful and inclusive participation of young people in disarmament and non-proliferation discussions, in line with General Assembly resolution 78/31, including through youth-led initiatives and education and capacity-building programmes.

4.6	With regard to inter-agency coordination and liaison, and in order to enhance delivery, support coherence and avoid the duplication of efforts, the Office will continue to pursue cohesive and integrated approaches across the Secretariat and resident coordinators and with United Nations agencies, funds and programmes and other United Nations entities, such as the United Nations Youth Office, the Office for Digital and Emerging Technologies and the Coordinating Action on Small Arms mechanism, which is the United Nations system-wide inter-agency coordination mechanism for small arms and ammunition control and the reduction in armed violence. This will help to ensure the promotion of a holistic approach to addressing risks associated with arms and to ensure the greater integration of disarmament, conflict prevention, humanitarian and development efforts, thereby contributing to the protection of civilians and international peace and security. These arrangements have generated efficiency gains by leveraging shared expertise and networks, enabling the Office to maximize impact.
4.7	With regard to the external factors, the overall plan for 2027 is based on the following assumptions:
(a)	The political environment and developments are supportive of disarmament efforts and are conducive to the facilitation of negotiations on new arms limitation and disarmament agreements;

(b)	Member States continue to engage with multilateral solutions for disarmament, non-proliferation and arms control and are willing to implement the recommendations, resolutions and decisions of relevant meetings and conferences;
(c)	International and regional organizations and entities and relevant stakeholders are interested and engaged in advancing and supporting disarmament, non-proliferation and arms control objectives;

(d)	National institutions support the promotion of equal opportunities for women and of the meaningful and inclusive participation and engagement of young people in disarmament, non-proliferation and arms control;
(e)	The Office has adequate resources for the implementation of planned activities.

4.8	The Office integrates a gender perspective into its activities, deliverables and results, as appropriate, in line with organizational mandates for mainstreaming a gender perspective into policies and programmes in the United Nations system (Economic and Social Council resolution 2024/3). The Office will continue to assist Member States, upon request, in implementing the Security Council resolutions on women and peace and security and, in line with General Assembly resolution 65/69 and subsequent resolutions on women, disarmament, non-proliferation and arms control, promote the role of women in these fields, and promote gender-sensitive policymaking and programming, including through data collection and education.

4.9	In line with the United Nations Disability Inclusion Strategy and General Assembly resolutions 74/144 and 76/154, the Office will continue to implement its departmental action plan and collaborate with other United Nations entities to promote accessibility and the equal participation of persons with disabilities in disarmament discussions and decision-making processes.
Adjustments arising from the UN80 Initiative

4.10	The proposed programme plan for 2027 reflects changes arising from the UN80 Initiative. In particular, the tables of deliverables reflect updated deliverables for 2026, in line with the approved programme budget for 2026.

4.11	The Office will be guided by any future decisions taken in the context of the UN80 Initiative, in accordance with the applicable rules and procedures, including decisions, resolutions and established practice of the competent intergovernmental organs. This includes, among others, any decisions concerning proposals to integrate the United Nations Institute for Disarmament Research into the Office so that its analysis informs negotiations and field support, while preserving the Institute’s research independence.

children.text: Evaluation activities
4.14	The following evaluations are planned for 2027:
(a)	An Office of Internal Oversight Services evaluation of the Office, with a specific focus area to be determined;

(b)	An evaluation conducted by the Office of the small arms and light weapons fellowship training programme covering its design, delivery and contributions to strengthening regional and national small arms and light weapons control systems.
Programme of work
Subprogramme 1 – Multilateral negotiations and deliberations on disarmament and arms limitation
Objective

4.15	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to advance multilateral negotiations and deliberations on agreements on disarmament, arms limitation and non-proliferation in all its aspects, as required by States Parties to existing multilateral agreements and the Conference on Disarmament.
Strategy
4.16	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Provide support for negotiations in the Conference on Disarmament and in conferences and meetings of States Parties to various multilateral agreements on disarmament, arms limitation and non-proliferation in all its aspects at the organizational, procedural and substantive levels;

(b)	Render assistance and provide support for the follow-up to decisions, recommendations and programmes of action adopted at conferences and meetings of States Parties to various multilateral disarmament, arms control and non-proliferation agreements and to treaty-making conferences, expert groups and other disarmament meetings under the responsibility of the subprogramme;
(c)	Facilitate the efforts of Member States to prevent an arms race in outer space.
4.17	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	Effective and efficient holding of the meetings of the Conference on Disarmament, other conferences, working groups and expert groups and the various treaty bodies falling under the remit of the subprogramme;

(b)	Strengthened operational viability and compliance with treaty obligations and adherence to and/or implementation of decisions, recommendations and programmes of action, as well as increased reporting by States Parties.
Programme performance in 2025

Increased annual reporting by High Contracting Parties to the Convention on Prohibitions or Restrictions on the Use of Certain Conventional Weapons Which May Be Deemed to Be Excessively Injurious or to Have Indiscriminate Effects

4.18	The High Contracting Parties to the Convention on Prohibitions or Restrictions on the Use of Certain Conventional Weapons Which May Be Deemed to Be Excessively Injurious or to Have Indiscriminate Effects submit national annual reports on compliance, on the implementation of Amended Protocol II and on the implementation of Protocol V. The subprogramme has undertaken efforts in recent years to promote the implementation of the Convention, including by facilitating increased reporting by the High Contracting Parties, in accordance with the final document of the sixth Review Conference of the Convention.

children.text: Result 3: addressing emerging technologies in the area of lethal autonomous weapons systems – Proposed programme plan for 2027

4.24	The open-ended Group of Governmental Experts on Emerging Technologies in the Area of Lethal Autonomous Weapons, convened within the framework of the Convention on Prohibitions or Restrictions on the Use of Certain Conventional Weapons Which May Be Deemed to Be Excessively Injurious or to Have Indiscriminate Effects, has considered issues related to emerging technologies in the area of lethal autonomous weapons systems. Since the establishment of the Group of Governmental Experts in 2016, the subprogramme has provided substantive and procedural support, including by facilitating the work of the Group, including its consideration and formulation of a set of elements of an instrument, without prejudging its nature, and other possible measures to address emerging technologies in the area of lethal autonomous weapons systems.
Lessons learned and planned change

4.25	The lesson for the subprogramme was that the continuity of substantive support to the Group of Governmental Experts on Emerging Technologies in the Area of Lethal Autonomous Weapons could help to support the advancement of deliberations for the formulation of a set of elements of an instrument and other possible measures to address emerging technologies in the area of lethal autonomous weapons systems. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will intensify efforts, including by facilitating the timely payment of assessed contributions for multilateral disarmament conventions, to support implementation of the consolidated financial measures agreed at the sixth Review Conference (CCW/CONF.VI/11, annex III), in order to ensure the operational continuity of the Convention on Prohibitions or Restrictions on the Use of Certain Conventional Weapons Which May Be Deemed to Be Excessively Injurious or to Have Indiscriminate Effect.

children.text: Subprogramme 2 – Weapons of mass destruction
Objective

4.28	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to advance disarmament and non-proliferation in all aspects of nuclear weapons and all other weapons of mass destruction and their means of delivery.
Strategy
4.29	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Support the process of negotiations, deliberations and consensus-building in the areas of disarmament and non-proliferation of weapons of mass destruction, in particular nuclear weapons and their means of delivery;

(b)	Support the full implementation of international instruments dealing with the disarmament and non-proliferation of nuclear weapons, including the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons and the Treaty on the Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons, and for the entry into force of the Comprehensive Nuclear-Test-Ban Treaty;
(c)	Support the enhancement and implementation of further ways and means of cooperation among the States Parties to nuclear-weapon-free zone treaties and the protocols thereto;
(d)	Support Member States in their negotiations on the establishment of a Middle East zone free of nuclear weapons and all other weapons of mass destruction;
(e)	Provide substantive and technical support to office bearers of the First Committee and the Disarmament Commission;

(f)	Continue to raise awareness of the dangers posed by nuclear weapons, including through substantive and technical support to the International Day for the Total Elimination of Nuclear Weapons and the International Day against Nuclear Tests;

(g)	Strengthen cooperation with relevant intergovernmental organizations, such as the International Atomic Energy Agency, including in areas such as nuclear security and non-proliferation, and expand interaction, as appropriate, with civil society, in particular research and academic institutions and non-governmental organizations working in the areas of disarmament and non-proliferation of nuclear weapons and their delivery systems;

(h)	Continue its close cooperation with the Organisation for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons (OPCW) to promote the universality and full implementation of the Convention on the Prohibition of the Development, Production, Stockpiling and Use of Chemical Weapons and on Their Destruction;

(i)	Support efforts to ensure accountability for the use of chemical weapons by, inter alia, supporting OPCW efforts in this regard and by working with members of the Security Council in order to build unity on the issue. The subprogramme will also continue to provide briefings to the Council on the implementation of resolution 2118 (2013) on the elimination of the chemical weapons programme of the Syrian Arab Republic;

(j)	Continue to enhance the operational readiness of the Secretary-General’s Mechanism for Investigation of Alleged Use of Chemical and Biological Weapons by organizing, with interested Member States and relevant partners, training and capacity-building activities for experts and analytical laboratories.
4.30	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	The building of trust and understanding among Member States in order to facilitate consensus on effective measures relating to the total elimination of nuclear weapons and measures to prevent any use of a nuclear weapon and to uphold the norms against the proliferation and testing of nuclear weapons;
(b)	The strengthening and reinforcement of the norm against chemical weapons use;
(c)	Enhanced ability of Member States to uphold the global norms against the use of weapons of mass destruction;
(d)	Strengthened coordination capabilities of Member States to respond to incidents involving the alleged use of biological weapons.
Programme performance in 2025
Member States from the Asia-Pacific region identify potential synergies for their engagement with instruments for addressing the threat posed by the proliferation or use of biological weapons

4.31	The subprogramme co-organized, with subprogramme 1, a workshop for Member States from the Asia-Pacific region (South-East Asia and Pacific subregions) to engage regionally on the global norm against biological weapons, in the context of the Biological Weapons Convention, Security Council resolution 1540 (2004) and the Secretary-General’s Mechanism for Investigation of Alleged Use of Chemical and Biological Weapons. These three instruments all contribute to preventing and deterring the proliferation or use of biological weapons and can benefit from cooperation with similar stakeholders at the national and regional levels. Building on a pilot workshop, held in 2024, for Member States of Latin American and the Caribbean, the subprogramme brought together national stakeholders, including those whose work is relevant to more than one of these instruments, thereby facilitating opportunities for Member States to identify potential synergies in their engagement on these issues.

Result 1: process to achieve the establishment of a Middle East zone free of nuclear weapons and other weapons of mass destruction – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

4.33	The work of the subprogramme contributed to the completion of the sixth session of the Conference on the Establishment of a Middle East Zone Free of Nuclear Weapons and Other Weapons of Mass Destruction, with the President of the sixth session, together with the members of the Conference, undertaking substantive deliberations during the annual session of the Conference and at its intersessional working committee on a number of issues, including: (a) consultation, clarification and cooperation, as well as dispute settlement; (b) negative security assurances; and (c) promoting cooperation with other nuclear-weapons-free zones. The deliberations were aimed at reaching a common position on the negotiation of a Middle East zone treaty on those issues, which met the planned target.

children.text: Result 2: improved readiness of the Secretary-General’s Mechanism for Investigation of Alleged Use of Chemical and Biological Weapons – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

4.35	The subprogramme’s work contributed to 80 trained experts who were nominated to the roster of the Secretary-General’s Mechanism for Investigation of Alleged Use of Chemical and Biological Weapons, which exceeded the planned target of 75.

Performance measure: number of trained experts on the Mechanism’s roster (annual)

Result 3: Group of Scientific and Technical Experts on Nuclear Disarmament Verification advances understanding of technical challenges in and possible approaches to nuclear disarmament verification – Proposed programme plan for 2027

4.37	To help to advance disarmament and the non-proliferation of nuclear weapons in all its aspects, the subprogramme convened three informal in-person meetings in 2025 on the technical challenges in and possible approaches to nuclear disarmament verification. The subprogramme also prepared a report of the Secretary-General to the General Assembly containing possible options for the establishment of a Group of Scientific and Technical Experts on Nuclear Disarmament Verification within the United Nations, taking into account the views submitted by Member States in writing and through the three informal in-person meetings as the basis for the subprogramme’s efforts to support the establishment and work of the Group, as mandated by the Assembly in its resolution 80/53. The subprogramme is also mandated, by this resolution, to provide all assistance necessary to the Group, including the provision of relevant documents.
Lessons learned and planned change

4.38	The lesson for the subprogramme, informed by previous experience of providing support to groups of experts, was the importance of establishing clear working methods and shared expectations at the outset of mandated activities of groups of experts. Early agreement on roles and responsibilities, decision-making modalities, timelines and communication channels contributes to more efficient deliberations, helps to reduce procedural delays and enables experts to focus on the substantive aspects of the mandate. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will support the development of standardized orientation briefings, model terms of reference for internal working methods and structured workplans aligned with mandated deliverables. The subprogramme will also facilitate early consultations among experts to foster a common understanding of objectives and outputs.

Subprogramme 3 – Conventional arms
Objective

4.41	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to advance the regulation and limitation of conventional weapons and ammunition, taking into account the legitimate needs of States for self-defence.
Strategy
4.42	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:
(a)	Provide substantive support, including preparatory activities for intergovernmental processes, to the following:
(i)	First Committee of the General Assembly and the Security Council;

(ii)	Preparations for Biennial Meeting of States to Consider the Implementation of the Programme of Action to Prevent, Combat and Eradicate the Illicit Trade in Small Arms and Light Weapons in All Its Aspects and its open-ended technical expert group to address developments in small arms and light weapons manufacturing, technologies and design;
(iii)	Meeting of States on the Global Framework for Through-life Conventional Ammunition Management;
(b)	Support and assist with the development, updating, revision, translation and dissemination, as appropriate, of the following:
(i)	International Ammunition Technical Guidelines through the SaferGuard programme;
(ii)	Database on military expenditure;
(iii)	Register of Conventional Arms;
(iv)	Modular Small-arms-control Implementation Compendium;
(v)	Repository of military confidence-building measures;
(vi)	Database on national reports of the Programme of Action on Small Arms and Light Weapons;
(vii)	Online platform and reporting database related to the Global Framework for Through-life Conventional Ammunition Management;

(viii)	Development, design, regionalization and coordination of the dedicated fellowship training programme on small arms and light weapons control and the alignment of a training curriculum with the Programme of Action on Small Arms and Light Weapons, the International Tracing Instrument and other relevant instruments and tools, including the Modular Small-arms-control Implementation Compendium;

(ix)	Development, design, regionalization and coordination of the fellowship training programme on through-life conventional ammunition management and alignment of a training curriculum with the Global Framework for Through-life Conventional Ammunition Management and other relevant instruments and tools, including the International Ammunition Technical Guidelines;

(c)	Implement and assist States in the implementation of the outcomes of the fourth United Nations Conference to Review Progress Made in the Implementation of the Programme of Action on Small Arms and Light Weapons;
(d)	Assist States in the regional and national implementation of the Global Framework for Through-life Conventional Ammunition Management;
(e)	Provide capacity-building support for requesting States to collect and consolidate data to monitor indicator 16.4.2 of the Sustainable Development Goals;
(f)	In partnership with United Nations system entities:
(i)	Promote safe and secure weapons and ammunition management in disarmament, demobilization and reintegration processes and in peace operation contexts;
(ii)	Support the integration of small arms control considerations into United Nations country-level system-wide approaches to development;

(g)	Support, implement and facilitate assistance projects that develop the capacity of national institutions with regard to conventional arms and ammunition control, including through (i) the United Nations Trust Facility Supporting Cooperation on Arms Regulation, (ii) the Saving Lives Entity fund, (iii) the SaferGuard Programme,  (iii) the maintenance and expansion of the roster of experts validated according to the International Ammunition Technical Guidelines, (iv) the establishment and operationalization of an efficient and agile global mechanism for requesting, offering and receiving assistance in through-life conventional ammunition management, and (v) a structured procedure to process offers of and requests for assistance submitted under the Programme of Action on Small Arms and Light Weapons and the International Tracing Instrument; support the continued work of the Ammunition Management Advisory Team; and support Silencing the Guns by 2030 and its Africa Amnesty Month initiative of the African Union;

(h)	Implement, as required, relevant recommendations issued in 2025 by the Group of Governmental Experts on the United Nations Register of Conventional Arms and undertake preparations for the meetings of the Group to be held in 2027 and 2028;
(i)	Manage the transparency instruments on military expenditure and the Register of Conventional Arms and provide support to States for their increased participation and use;
(j)	Support initiatives and efforts for fostering military confidence-building measures at the subregional and regional levels;

(k)	Support efforts to combat, prevent and eradicate the illicit trade in small arms and light weapons, their parts and ammunition in accordance with actions 18 (f) and 26 (g) of the Pact for the Future;

(l)	Support the efforts of Member States, pursuant to their request, to help to ensure that military spending does not compromise investment in sustainable development and building sustainable peace, consistent with measures contained in the report of the Secretary-General entitled The Security We Need: Rebalancing Military Spending for a Sustainable and Peaceful Future.
4.43	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	Strengthened implementation of global arms control instruments, including legislation, policies and regulations on small arms and light weapons and conventional ammunition at the national, regional and subregional levels;

(b)	Enhanced effectiveness of existing international assistance frameworks through the implementation of the Programme of Action on Small Arms and Light Weapons, the International Tracing Instrument and the Global Framework for Through-life Conventional Ammunition Management;
(c)	Improved integration of small arms and light weapons and conventional ammunition control considerations into development and prevention processes and frameworks;

(d)	Reduced armed violence and risk of armed conflict by reducing the destabilizing effect of increasing arms and ammunition transfers and accumulations, as well as fostering confidence-building measures among States;
(e)	Continued relevance and improved participation of States in the United Nations Report on Military Expenditures and the Register of Conventional Arms.
Programme performance in 2025
Increased understanding of and proposals to mitigate the impact of the global increase in military expenditure on progress towards the achievement of the Sustainable Development Goals

4.44	Through the Pact for the Future, Member States expressed concern about the potential impact that the global increase in military expenditure could have on investment in sustainable development and sustaining peace. To take this forward, the subprogramme, in collaboration with the Executive Office of the Secretary-General and the United Nations Development Programme, undertook consultations with relevant United Nations entities, international financial institutions, Member States, academia, experts and civil society. That input informed the report of the Secretary-General, The Security We Need: Rebalancing Military Spending for a Sustainable and Peaceful Future, which provided an analysis on the impact of the global increase in military expenditure on the achievement of the Sustainable Development Goals. The Secretary-General, in his report, articulated that higher levels of military expenditure did not always lead to greater stability or peace and that growing military expenditure was crowding out resources for sustainable development. The Secretary-General made the case for investing in peace and set out an agenda to mitigate the impact of the global increase in military expenditure on the progress towards the achievement of the Goals. To support broad dissemination of the research and the report, a dedicated outreach campaign and web page were launched to engage Member States and the wider public.

children.text: Result 3: strengthened expertise to advance implementation of the Programme of Action on Small Arms and Light Weapons and its International Tracing Instrument – Proposed programme plan for 2027

4.50	Since 2024, the subprogramme has designed, developed and helped to operationalize the small arms and light weapons fellowship training programme as a standing initiative to strengthen the technical and institutional capacity of Member States to implement the Programme of Action on Small Arms and Light Weapons and the International Tracing Instrument. In 2025, the subprogramme tailored the curricula to regional contexts and coordinated the implementation of two regional sessions, in Latin American and the Caribbean and in Africa, with support from subprogramme 4, resulting in the successful completion of the training of government officials and practitioners, combining technical modules on marking, tracing and stockpile management with practical exercises and idea incubation.
Lessons learned and planned change

4.51	The lesson for the subprogramme was the importance of embedding structured mentorship and sustained post-training engagement within the programme design. While fellows actively participated and developed project proposals, follow‑up mechanisms for implementation and reporting would benefit from more structured engagement and coaching. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will strengthen the mentorship and coaching approaches of the fellows. This will include follow-up and impact‑tracking components, ensuring that fellows receive technical assistance to refine and operationalize their individual projects to contribute to national reporting and implementation regarding the Programme of Action on Small Arms and Light Weapons and its International Tracing Instrument. These changes are designed to deepen the fellowship’s effectiveness and foster tangible improvements in control practices regarding small arms and light weapons at the institutional and policy levels.

Subprogramme 4 – Regional disarmament, capacity-building and outreach
Objective

4.54	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to advance the understanding and expertise of Member States and the public for the attainment of disarmament, non-proliferation and arms control goals and to ensure cooperation at the global and regional levels on disarmament, non-proliferation and arms control.
Strategy
4.55	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Undertake outreach and information‑sharing activities using websites, databases and communication platforms, in cooperation with Member States and relevant stakeholders, including organizing events, panel discussions, exhibitions, media engagement and public communications and producing publications tailored to diverse audiences;

(b)	Support civil society engagement and promote the meaningful and inclusive participation and empowerment of young people in intergovernmental forums by facilitating access arrangements, organizing consultative meetings and arranging for presentations by invited stakeholders, as well as through a dedicated digital platform Youth for Disarmament and token grants and awards supported by voluntary contributions;

(c)	Implement the recommendations of the 2002 United Nations study on disarmament and non-proliferation education, in cooperation with Member States and other relevant partners, by producing and disseminating educational materials and developing and maintaining e-_learning and other online educational resources on substantive topics for fellowships and for the general public, including relevant hosting and maintenance requirements, with due regard given to multilingualism and accessibility;

(d)	Provide the support necessary to operationalize all aspects of the global mechanism on developments in the field of information and communications technology (ICT) in the context of international security, pursuant to General Assembly resolution 80/16;
(e)	Facilitate and support the conduct of training activities for the implementation of mandated fellowship capacity-building programmes;

(f)	Provide technical and legal training, conduct capacity-building activities and support the implementation by Member States of relevant treaties, international standards and guidelines, including the Programme of Action on Small Arms and Light Weapons in All Its Aspects, the Arms Trade Treaty, the Biological Weapons Convention, Security Council resolutions 1325 (2000), 1540 (2004), 1673 (2006), 1810 (2008), 1977 (2011), 2055 (2012), 2325 (2016), 2370 (2017), 2572 (2021), 2622 (2022) and 2663 (2022) and General Assembly resolutions 79/57, 80/60, 80/61 and 80/62.
4.56	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Increased understanding, expertise and informed engagement of Member States on disarmament, non-proliferation and arms control issues;

(b)	Increased interest in and support for multilateral disarmament efforts and enhanced engagement by the general public and other stakeholders on disarmament, non-proliferation and arms control issues, as appropriate and as mandated;

(c)	Progress in the advancement of multilateral deliberations by States on disarmament, non-proliferation and arms control issues, including ICT security in the context of international security leading to common understandings among States;

(d)	Increased adherence to the disarmament instruments and strengthened small arms, light weapons and ammunition control, thereby advancing regional and international peace and security, including curbing the illicit trade in and the circulation of small arms and light weapons;
(e)	Confidence-building measures among Member States;
(f)	Cooperation with regional and subregional organizations.
Programme performance in 2025
Strengthened national disarmament expertise for advancing disarmament efforts

4.57	The subprogramme provided blended learning opportunities, which may include study tours, online instruction, lectures, simulations and in-person training across regions, through the implementation of the United Nations Disarmament Fellowship, Training and Advisory Services Programme and the United Nations programme of fellowships on small arms and light weapons. These fellowships focus on strengthening practical knowledge, technical skills and specialized expertise for disarmament, non-proliferation and arms control goals, instruments and processes and on strengthening awareness of the value of disarmament, enabling fellows to engage more effectively in disarmament efforts at all levels. Through this structured approach, the subprogramme fosters a growing global network of practitioners that enhances international cooperation and provides sustained support to the development and implementation of multilateral disarmament and arms control agreements and instruments.

Result 1: Global Mechanism on developments in the field of information and communications technologies in the context of international security and advancing responsible State behaviour in the use of information and communication technologies – Proposed programme plan for 2027

4.59	The subprogramme provides support to the open-ended working group on security of and in the use of information and communications technologies 2021–2025 in the context of international security in the successful fulfilment of its mandate. Through General Assembly resolution 80/16, States endorsed the final report of the open-ended working group and requested the Secretary-General to provide the support necessary to operationalize all aspects of the Global Mechanism, starting in 2026.
Lessons learned and planned change

4.60	The lesson for the subprogramme was that early planning and financial support for delegations could significantly improve both attendance and meaningful and inclusive participation of States in intergovernmental discussions on ICT in the context of international security. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will undertake outreach to identify potential barriers faced by delegations’ participation and intensify its efforts to support programmes that facilitate the participation of delegates in intergovernmental discussions, including sponsorship programmes. In addition, the subprogramme will support delegations, pursuant to their request, with the provision of informal briefings and background materials to advance the work of the Global Mechanism.
